input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. This avoids having to materialize the list a second time with something like ``items|list|length`` just to display a group's size. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if with_counts: out = [] for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr): group_list = await auto_to_list(group) out.append((key, group_list, len(group_list))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(group)) for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ]

content.text: Now let's verify it compiles and test quickly.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [dict(name='a', city='NY'), dict(name='b', city='NY'), dict(name='c', city='LA')]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Both sync cases work. Now test async variant.

input.command:
python -c "
import asyncio
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment(enable_async=True)
tmpl = env.from_string('{% for city, items, count in users|groupby(\"city\", with_counts=True) %}{{ city }}:{{ count }} {% endfor %}')
users = [dict(name='a', city='NY'), dict(name='b', city='NY'), dict(name='c', city='LA')]
print(asyncio.run(tmpl.render_async(users=users)))
tmpl2 = env.from_string('{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}')
print(asyncio.run(tmpl2.render_async(users=users)))
"

content.text: Both work correctly. Let's run the existing groupby tests to ensure nothing broke, then check for messages from agent1.
All existing tests pass. Now let's run the full filter test suite as a sanity check and check inbox for agent1.

content.content:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. This avoids having to materialize the list a second time with something like ``items|list|length`` just to display a group's size. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if with_counts: out = [] for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr): group_list = await auto_to_list(group) out.append((key, group_list, len(group_list))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(group)) for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ]

tool_use_result.stdout:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``with_counts=True`` to also get the number of items in each group, yielding ``(grouper, list, count)`` tuples instead. This avoids having to materialize the list a second time with something like ``items|list|length`` just to display a group's size. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items, count in users|groupby("city", with_counts=True) %}
  • {{ city }} ({{ count }})
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``with_counts`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) if with_counts: out = [] for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr): values = list(values) out.append((key, values, len(values))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, with_counts: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, ...]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) values = await auto_to_list(value) if with_counts: out = [] for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr): group_list = await auto_to_list(group) out.append((key, group_list, len(group_list))) return out return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(group)) for key, group in groupby(sorted(values, key=expr), expr) ]

Implementation is complete:

- Added `with_counts: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and the async `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `with_counts=True`, yields `(grouper, items, count)` tuples computed during the single grouping pass (no extra materialization pass); default behavior (`with_counts=False`) is unchanged, preserving the existing `_GroupTuple(grouper, list)` namedtuple output for backward compatibility.
- Updated the docstring with a usage example and a `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually with sync and async render tests, and confirmed all existing tests (`tests/test_filters.py`, groupby-specific tests) still pass.

Patch written to `/workspace/repo/patch.txt`, containing only the `with_counts` changes to `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
